jokes.text: The TGS sweatshirts were supposed to say 'TGS Season Six, Yuck,' which is a catch-phrase from a pretty great sketch. Yuck! But they got misprinted and now they say, 'TGS Season Six, Duck!' And that's a season five catchphrase. Duck!?
jokes.setup: Misprinted sweatshirts seem like a minor problem
jokes.punchline: The specific absurdity of wrong season catchphrases with deadpan delivery
jokes.explanation: Classic 30 Rock meta-humor about their own show's fictional elements. The escalating seriousness about trivial details is perfectly Liz.

jokes.text: A real problem is losing your giant scissors right before a ribbon-cutting for a couch factory. I just...had them.
jokes.setup: Jack dismisses Liz's problem as not real
jokes.punchline: His 'real problem' is absurdly specific and privileged - giant scissors for couch factory
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack Donaghy absurdity - his wealth problems are so specific and ridiculous. The bewildered delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Oh, thank God. I thought I'd lost them.
jokes.setup: Jack was looking for his giant scissors
jokes.punchline: Apparently finds them immediately after claiming to lose them
jokes.explanation: Quick visual payoff to the scissors setup. Economical but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: factories provide three things this country desperately needs: Jobs, pride, and material for Bruce Springsteen songs.
jokes.setup: Jack starts with serious economic commentary
jokes.punchline: Third item is absurdly specific cultural reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from serious to absurd. The Bruce Springsteen reference is unexpected and specific enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: Oh, I listened to rock and roll music once, but I stopped before I started worshipping the devil. The song went, 'Mr. Sandma--', then I shut it off.
jokes.setup: Kenneth admits to listening to rock music once
jokes.punchline: He stopped mid-'Mr. Sandman' thinking it was devil worship
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth character moment. His extreme religious naivety combined with the innocuous 'Mr. Sandman' is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: Back in Chicago, I walked in on you with the guy from blues traveler. He still had his harmonica thing on.
jokes.setup: Liz recalls Jenna with musicians before
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of the harmonica harness during sex
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor elevated by the specific detail. The harmonica harness is a perfect uncomfortable visual that's both specific and believable for Jenna.

jokes.text: Like Yoko Ono and the Beatles, or Lance Drake Mandrell and Wilson Phillips.
jokes.setup: Jenna compares herself to Yoko Ono
jokes.punchline: Adds fake obscure reference 'Lance Drake Mandrell and Wilson Phillips'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from real reference to made-up one. Shows Jenna's tendency to embellish and make herself seem more knowledgeable.

jokes.text: They sold out Madison square garden in nine minutes, for a Tuesday morning show. Wait, who plays on a Tuesday morning?
jokes.setup: Jenna brags about the band's popularity selling out MSG
jokes.punchline: Liz's deadpan realization that Tuesday morning shows are clearly for children
jokes.explanation: Nice beat where Liz's practical mind cuts through Jenna's delusion. The timing of the realization is well-crafted.

jokes.text: I don't know, just a little band called the Woggels. ♪ apples are red ♪ ♪ bananas are yellow ♪ ♪ I love my friends, bananas are yellow ♪
jokes.setup: Jenna dramatically reveals the band name
jokes.punchline: Hilariously simple children's song with repeated 'bananas are yellow' line
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Jenna's serious delivery and the absurdly simple children's song is perfect. The repetitive lyrics are memorably dumb.

jokes.text: My eight-year-old niece walked down the aisle to that song at her wedding.
jokes.setup: Kenneth recognizes the Woggels song
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions his eight-year-old niece's wedding
jokes.explanation: Perfectly delivered Kenneth absurdism. The casual mention of child marriage is delivered with such matter-of-fact tone that it becomes surreal and hilarious.

jokes.text: Well, it ticks off a lot of boxes on my sexual walkabout list. 'Yoko a band, make love to a beloved children's entertainer, be with a non-aboriginal Australian--'
jokes.setup: Jenna explains her motivations for dating Russ
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and problematic checklist items
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency for Jenna's narcissistic approach to relationships. The escalating specificity of her list is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Maybe Raffi. Or the sedated prisoner they put inside of Barney.
jokes.setup: Jenna hopes Paul is also with a children's entertainer
jokes.punchline: Escalates from innocent Raffi to dark Barney conspiracy theory
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from wholesome to dark. The 'sedated prisoner' theory about Barney is memorably twisted and unexpected.

jokes.text: Something terrible has happened! And for once I'm not talking about the collapse of the middle class.
jokes.setup: Tracy announces something terrible
jokes.punchline: Clarifies it's not his usual obsession with economic inequality
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Tracy's unexpected political awareness as a running trait.

jokes.text: I didn't even get in, and I was first in my class. Although, only seven of us graduated that year because of a bad mono outbreak which somehow missed me, despite all the frenching I did.
jokes.setup: Liz was first in her class but didn't get into Stanford
jokes.punchline: Reveals the pathetically small class size, then overshares about 'frenching'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz moment - starts with modest boasting, deflates it, then overshares awkwardly. The escalation works well.

jokes.text: We go to the school of hard knocks, a one-year vocational program where you learn to bang on doors and scare people into subscribing to magazines that they'll never get.
jokes.setup: Tracy explains Jordan family tradition of 'school of hard knocks'
jokes.punchline: Treats the metaphor literally as an actual vocational program with specific curriculum
jokes.explanation: Brilliant literalization of the 'school of hard knocks' metaphor. The specific details of the curriculum are perfectly absurd and criminal.

jokes.text: Really, Dotcom? How did your city council campaign go? Well, as far as raising the level of discourse in this city? I'd say it was a great success.
jokes.setup: Tracy challenges Dotcom about needing college
jokes.punchline: Dotcom's diplomatic response clearly indicates he lost badly
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat showing Dotcom's dignity in defeat. The euphemistic response is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Born April 24, 1925, at Boston catholic guilt hospital.
jokes.setup: Doctor confirming patient identity with birthplace
jokes.punchline: Hospital name is 'Boston Catholic Guilt Hospital'
jokes.explanation: Clever institutional name that perfectly captures both Boston and Catholic cultural stereotypes in one economical phrase.

jokes.text: We found your name on a list of disappointments she keeps folded up in her shoe.
jokes.setup: Doctor needs someone to release patient to
jokes.punchline: Found Jack's name on mother's 'list of disappointments' in her shoe
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist detail. The image of a formal 'list of disappointments' kept in a shoe is perfectly Colleen and hilariously specific.

jokes.text: Dr. Singh, are you stealing my hats? What do you do, feed them to your cow God?
jokes.setup: Colleen addresses the doctor
jokes.punchline: Casually racist assumption about Hindu practices
jokes.explanation: Establishes Colleen's casual racism character trait. More uncomfortable than funny, but serves character development.

jokes.text: Ooh, sofa. Somebody got all flouncy when I wasn't looking. What are you gonna do, put on your galoshes and go eat some fruit, like a Frenchman?
jokes.setup: Colleen learns Jack is opening a sofa factory
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific emasculating stereotype about French fruit-eating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Colleen insult - combines anti-French sentiment with bizarre specificity about galoshes and fruit. The absurdity makes it memorable.

jokes.text: What are you gonna do, put on your galoshes and go eat some fruit, like a Frenchman?
jokes.setup: Colleen continues mocking Jack
jokes.punchline: Bizarre stereotype about French people eating fruit in galoshes
jokes.explanation: Absurdist insult that makes no logical sense but perfectly captures Colleen's ability to make everything sound shameful.

jokes.text: Yes, I know it's over, Kouchie. Unlike you, I have eyes.
jokes.setup: Doctor tries to manage difficult conversation
jokes.punchline: Colleen makes cruel reference to doctor's vision/appearance
jokes.explanation: More cruel than clever. Establishes character but lacks the wit that makes other Colleen moments memorable.

jokes.text: Nothing can tear us apart... Except for the shark that got a hold of the fifth woggel.
jokes.setup: Ian declares nothing can tear the band apart
jokes.punchline: Immediately mentions shark attack that killed fifth member
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor subverting the wholesome children's band setup. The casual mention of death-by-shark is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: My good friend dick Cheney got one. A month later, he and I went hunting in Mexico. He shot thousands of doves that day and only hit me in the leg once.
jokes.setup: Jack explains familiarity with LVAD devices through Dick Cheney
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions being shot by Cheney while hunting, treating it as minor inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack Donaghy moment - his casual mention of being shot by Dick Cheney while praising his marksmanship is brilliantly character-specific and absurd.

jokes.text: Like the flashlight in my race war preparedness bag.
jokes.setup: Jack compares LVAD batteries to other rechargeable devices
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions having a 'race war preparedness bag'
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny character moment revealing Jack's paranoid preparedness. The casual delivery makes the disturbing content funnier.

jokes.text: She's referring to my birth.
jokes.setup: Colleen describes Jack crying and wailing in hospital
jokes.punchline: Jack deadpans that she's talking about his birth
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery. The timing and understatement make this simple clarification very funny.

jokes.text: This is supposed to be a hospital, not a Japanese internment camp I volunteered at during the war.
jokes.setup: Colleen complains about hospital room quality
jokes.punchline: Compares it to internment camp she 'volunteered at'
jokes.explanation: Dark character moment establishing Colleen's problematic past. The casual delivery of horrific content is characteristic but disturbing.

jokes.text: You could look out at the park and watch carriage horses being whipped. I know how you love that.
jokes.setup: Jack offers to put mother up at the Plaza
jokes.punchline: Suggests she'd enjoy watching animal cruelty
jokes.explanation: Shows the toxic mother-son dynamic. Jack's casual cruelty mirrors his mother's, suggesting learned behavior.

jokes.text: What if I run into Eloise? Am I supposed not to mention the fact that she has a gay grandson? Everybody knows!
jokes.setup: Colleen explains why she can't go to the Plaza
jokes.punchline: Worried about gossiping about fictional character Eloise's gay grandson
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment treating fictional Eloise as real person with contemporary family drama. The specificity makes it memorable.

jokes.text: Because at Ellis island, your mother's name was recorded as 'unclaimed Irish stowaway.'
jokes.setup: Colleen asks why baby wasn't named after her mother
jokes.punchline: Jack reveals great-grandmother's official name was 'unclaimed Irish stowaway'
jokes.explanation: Excellent historical humor with perfect specificity. The bureaucratic dehumanization is both funny and historically plausible.

jokes.text: Son, we have a lot of work to do today if I'm gonna 'reverse Urkel' you. Dad, I just want to read. Jordan men don't read. Grizz, tear this book in half.
jokes.setup: Tracy wants to toughen up his intellectual son
jokes.punchline: Creates term 'reverse Urkel' and immediately destroys education
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy logic and the term 'reverse Urkel' is brilliant. The immediate book destruction is hilariously extreme.

jokes.text: Tray, montage got married and quit stripping.
jokes.setup: Tracy calls for 'montage time'
jokes.punchline: Grizz treats 'montage' as a person who got married and quit stripping
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist wordplay treating 'montage' as a person. The specific details about marriage and stripping make it memorable.

jokes.text: Next thing you know, they'll be giving her his retractable wings and rocket arms.
jokes.setup: Jack mentions Colleen getting the same device as Dick Cheney
jokes.punchline: Implies Cheney has cyborg features
jokes.explanation: Fun absurdist take on Cheney as cyborg. The escalation from heart pump to full robot features works well.

jokes.text: Lemon, my mother did explain sex to me, three years ago. There were...drawings.
jokes.setup: Liz mentions 'the talk,' Jack thinks she means sex education
jokes.punchline: Jack's mother explained sex to him at age ~45, with drawings
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy. The idea of a 45-year-old getting the birds and bees talk with visual aids is hilariously uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You talk in your sleep. I know what you did in the war, and before you die, I want you to know that I know.
jokes.setup: Liz explains how rewarding 'the talk' can be
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's confronting sleeping person about war crimes
jokes.explanation: Dark and unexpected. Liz's casual mention of confronting someone about war crimes is perfectly absurd and disturbing.

jokes.text: like Bill Belichick listening to Adele.
jokes.setup: Jack describes emotional sobbing
jokes.punchline: Compares it to stoic football coach listening to emotional music
jokes.explanation: Great visual comedy in the contrast between notoriously stoic Belichick and emotional Adele music. Economical and specific.

jokes.text: As my coffee cup said this morning, 'you only regret the things you didn't do. This cup was made from recycled toilet paper.'
jokes.setup: Jack quotes inspirational message from coffee cup
jokes.punchline: Cup's second message reveals disturbing manufacturing details
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition between inspirational and gross. The specific detail about toilet paper recycling is unexpectedly funny.

jokes.text: And this is coming from someone Quincy Jones once pushed off a boat.
jokes.setup: Jenna compliments Russ's song
jokes.punchline: Establishes her musical credibility with bizarre specific anecdote
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna name-drop with bizarre twist. The image of Quincy Jones pushing someone off a boat is memorably specific.

jokes.text: It doesn't matter what this brain thinks. It matters what this brain thinks.
jokes.setup: Jenna tells Russ his opinion matters
jokes.punchline: Points to her head then to her crotch/body for 'brain'
jokes.explanation: Standard sexual innuendo elevated slightly by Jenna's character. Not particularly original but fits her manipulative nature.

jokes.text: Dean Cain is always there hoping to get recognized.
jokes.setup: Jack explains why Colleen won't go to the Princeton club
jokes.punchline: Random celebrity desperately seeking attention at exclusive club
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity reference humor. Dean Cain as pathetic fame-seeker is specific enough to be funny.

jokes.text: Can we do something about the temperature in here? I'd like it humid and cold. Duck!
jokes.setup: Colleen complains about temperature
jokes.punchline: Ends with 'Duck!' callback to the episode opening
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to opening joke. The temperature preference 'humid and cold' is amusingly specific and unpleasant.

jokes.text: In Australia, 'woggel' means 'white.'
jokes.setup: Ian appeals to 'Woggel power' to convince Russ
jokes.punchline: Russ reveals the band name has racist connotations
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy revealing the innocent children's band has problematic name origins. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: No, you don't know what you're talking about. Paul isn't gonna-- I mean, Russ. Paul is American for Russ.
jokes.setup: Ian suggests Russ will replace Jenna like she replaced girlfriends
jokes.punchline: Jenna accidentally reveals she's thinking about Paul, then covers poorly
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jenna's subconscious focus on Paul. The weak cover story 'Paul is American for Russ' is perfectly Jenna.

jokes.text: God on a wheel! Jackie and I know how we feel. We don't have to say it out loud like a couple of gays getting married in jean shorts in Provincetown while I'm just trying to enjoy an ice cream on the pier!
jokes.setup: Colleen rejects the idea of expressing feelings
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific rant about gay marriage disrupting her ice cream
jokes.explanation: Peak Colleen - the escalating specificity from emotional expression to ice cream disruption is perfectly character-driven absurdity.

jokes.text: 'Say it out, speak it clear. You should listen, dude. Also, hear. It's too late, when--' You have two minutes to find that battery before I die.
jokes.setup: Liz tries to quote Mike + The Mechanics song about communication
jokes.punchline: Butchers the lyrics horribly, then Colleen's battery emergency interrupts
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from Liz's failed inspiration to life-threatening urgency. The mangled lyrics are perfectly Liz-like.

jokes.text: Tony will rush it for me. We do zumba together.
jokes.setup: Grizz offers to expedite security paperwork
jokes.punchline: His connection is through dance fitness class
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Grizz's unexpected interests. The contrast with his tough appearance works well.

jokes.text: Like teaching him how to ride a bike... Down the Luxor pyramid. Or drive a car... Vel franchise into the ground. I never even taught him how to shave... An orangutan.
jokes.setup: Tracy lists normal father-son activities he missed
jokes.punchline: Each activity gets an absurd Tracy Jordan-specific twist
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating subversion of normal father-son activities. Each twist is perfectly Tracy-level absurd while maintaining the emotional core.

jokes.text: Hey, Angie. What's up? You had the baby? Okay. Name him George foreman. And also, we're out of soda at home. Lamont, hurry up and deliver that baby before they realize we're not doctors.
jokes.setup: Tracy takes a call about the baby being born
jokes.punchline: Reveals he wasn't at the birth and was impersonating a doctor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy moment - combines his neglect as a father with criminal activity. The escalation from mundane to illegal is expertly done.

jokes.text: All My Children... was cancelled.
jokes.setup: Dotcom tries to help but admits he's out of his wheelhouse
jokes.punchline: Reveals his expertise was in a cancelled soap opera
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dotcom's unexpected interests. The deadpan delivery of the cancellation news is well-timed.

jokes.text: ♪ separate the races, mommy kangaroo ♪
jokes.setup: Sweet children's song about baby kangaroo wanting to go home
jokes.punchline: Ends with shockingly racist lyric
jokes.explanation: Shocking and brilliant subversion. The innocent children's song format makes the racist content even more jarring and memorable.

jokes.text: Sew up your cabbage hole, girl.
jokes.setup: Colleen tells Liz to be quiet
jokes.punchline: Uses bizarre old-timey insult instead of modern language
jokes.explanation: Perfect character language showing Colleen's era and personality. 'Cabbage hole' is memorably disgusting.

jokes.text: If you were my kid, I'd mail you back to the stork.
jokes.setup: Colleen dismisses Liz's advice
jokes.punchline: Threatens to mail her back to the stork like returning merchandise
jokes.explanation: Perfect Colleen threat - combines maternal imagery with consumer returns. The absurdity of mailing someone to a stork is memorable.

jokes.text: When I was eight, she took me to the post office because I spilled juice on a couch reserved for the pope, which has still never been used. He'll come.
jokes.setup: Jack explains his mother actually tried to mail him back
jokes.punchline: Reveals absurd details about papal couch and his continued faith the Pope will visit
jokes.explanation: Excellent character backstory with escalating absurdity. The papal couch detail and Jack's continued hope is perfectly delusional.

jokes.text: which you should know was actually wine.
jokes.setup: Jack apologizes for spilling 'juice' on papal couch
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was actually wine, making 8-year-old drinking the real issue
jokes.explanation: Great reveal that recontextualizes the story. Child Jack drinking wine explains a lot about his upbringing and current personality.

jokes.text: Not a part of this, I get it. But I made this happen! I made this happen.
jokes.setup: Jack and Colleen have emotional mother-son reconciliation
jokes.punchline: Liz desperately claims credit while being completely ignored
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Liz's need for recognition even during others' emotional moments. The repetition shows her desperation.

jokes.text: because my tongue caught what my foot has.
jokes.setup: Tracy says this conversation isn't easy for him
jokes.punchline: Mangles expression about 'foot in mouth disease'
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy malapropism. The confused anatomy makes it memorable, though not his strongest wordplay moment.

jokes.text: Oh, God. He's dressed like her. I'm too late. I've been replaced.
jokes.setup: Jenna arrives to win Paul back
jokes.punchline: Sees Paul dressed like the woman he's apparently replaced her with
jokes.explanation: Good visual payoff to Jenna's fears. The cross-dressing reveal is unexpected but fits the show's absurdist tone.

jokes.text: Oh, you'd know. You lived through it.
jokes.setup: Colleen complains about Jack's questioning like 'the Spanish Inquisition'
jokes.punchline: Jack casually implies his ancient mother actually experienced it
jokes.explanation: Excellent age joke with perfect timing. The implication that Colleen is old enough to have lived through the Spanish Inquisition is absurdly cutting.

jokes.text: ♪ Feelings feelings feelings say how you feel ♪ ♪ Apples ♪ ♪ You taste good ♪ ♪ Trees ♪ ♪ You give us wood ♪ ♪ Grandma ♪ ♪ I am gay ♪ ♪ Bridge ♪ ♪ You turn me on in a sexual way ♪
jokes.setup: Woggels perform new song about expressing feelings
jokes.punchline: Song escalates from innocent to inappropriate with bridge sexual content
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from innocent children's song to completely inappropriate content. The bridge sexual attraction is a perfect inappropriate ending.
